title: How to Keep Cats Off Furniture Using a Cat Condo (2026 Guide)
seoTitle: Keep Cats Off Furniture With a Cat Condo | GetPawsy
metaDescription: Stop your cat from destroying furniture. Learn how a well-placed cat condo redirects scratching and climbing behavior naturally.
excerpt: Cats scratch furniture because they need vertical territory. A strategically placed cat condo solves the problem without sprays or punishment.

sections.heading: Why Cats Climb and Scratch Your Furniture
sections.content:
Cats aren't being destructive on purpose. Scratching serves three biological functions: claw maintenance (removing dead sheaths), territory marking (scent glands in paw pads), and stretching (engages shoulder and back muscles). Climbing satisfies their instinct to survey territory from an elevated vantage point.

When you don't provide appropriate outlets for these behaviors, your couch, curtains, and bookshelves become the default options. Punishment doesn't work — it only makes cats anxious, which often increases destructive behavior.

sections.heading: How a Cat Condo Solves the Problem
sections.content:
A well-chosen cat condo addresses every reason your cat targets furniture:

**Scratching surfaces**: Sisal-wrapped posts satisfy the urge to scratch far better than upholstery.

**Vertical territory**: Elevated platforms give cats the high vantage point they crave, making your bookshelf less appealing.

**Stretching space**: Tall posts allow full-body stretches that short scratching pads can't match.

**Comfort zones**: Enclosed hideaways provide the security cats seek when they nest in your clean laundry.

The key insight: you're not training your cat to stop a behavior. You're redirecting an innate need to a better location.

sections.heading: Strategic Placement for Maximum Effect
sections.content:
Where you place the cat condo is as important as which one you buy:

**Near the furniture they target**: Place the condo within 3 feet of the couch or chair your cat prefers. Once they consistently use the condo, gradually move it to your preferred location (no more than 6 inches per day).

**Near windows**: Cats are drawn to windows for visual stimulation. A condo by a window becomes the most attractive spot in the house.

**In social areas**: Cats want to be where the family is. A living room condo gets used more than one tucked in a spare bedroom.

**Away from litter boxes and food**: Cats don't want to perch near their bathroom or dining area.

sections.heading: Making the Transition
sections.content:
To accelerate adoption:

1. Rub catnip on the scratching posts (works for about 70% of cats)
2. Place treats on the platforms during the first week
3. Play with wand toys around the condo to create positive associations
4. Temporarily cover targeted furniture with double-sided tape (cats dislike the texture)
5. Praise and treat when your cat uses the condo independently

Most cats transition within 1-2 weeks. Persistent furniture scratchers may need supplemental scratching pads placed directly against their preferred spots.

sections.heading: Find the Right Cat Condo
sections.content: Browse our [cat condo collection](/collections/cat-condos) for models specifically designed with multiple scratching surfaces, elevated perches, and enclosed hideaways. All options ship free within the US on orders over $35 and include 30-day hassle-free returns.

faq.question: Will a cat condo definitely stop my cat from scratching furniture?
faq.answer: In most cases, yes — if the condo has adequate scratching surfaces and is placed strategically near the furniture your cat currently targets. About 80% of cats redirect within 2 weeks.

faq.question: What size cat condo do I need to keep my cat off counters?
faq.answer: Choose a condo at least as tall as your countertops (36+ inches). Cats jump on counters for the height advantage — give them a taller alternative.

faq.question: Should I still use deterrent sprays with a cat condo?
faq.answer: Deterrent sprays alone rarely work long-term. Pairing them with a cat condo (positive alternative) is more effective than sprays alone.
